steps.title: Understand what a margin of error means
steps.content: A survey reports an estimate (38%) with a margin of error (5.5%). The margin of error does NOT mean the estimate is exact — it defines a range of plausible values for the true population percentage.

steps.title: Compute the confidence interval
steps.content:
Apply the margin of error symmetrically around the estimate:
$$38\% - 5.5\% = 32.5\%$$
$$38\% + 5.5\% = 43.5\%$$
The plausible range is 32.5% to 43.5%.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• A — claims exactly 38% support the change. A margin of error means we cannot pin down an exact value; the true percent could be anywhere from 32.5% to 43.5%. This is too definitive.
  • B — claims the true percent is greater than 38%. The interval extends both above and below 38%, so values below 38% (e.g., 33%) are equally plausible.
  • C — states plausible values are between 32.5% and 43.5%. This is the precise meaning of a 5.5% margin of error around 38%. That matches Choice C — Plausible values of the percent of the students at the school who support a menu change are between 32.5% and 43.5%..
  • D — gives numbers of students (295–305) rather than percentages. 38% of 300 is about 114 students, not near 295–305. Wrong units and wrong values.
